analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 10 finding(s) from 10 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: External DMI/JTAG requests appear to reach dm_top debug controls without a visible jtag_unlock/lifecycle guard at the dm_top request boundary in riscv_peripherals.sv.
findings.vulnerability_category: issue DMI request/response transactions and trigger debug module controls / dm_top DMI-visible state and outputs ndmreset_o, dmactive_o, debug_req_o / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external JTAG/DTM debugger via tck_i/tms_i/td_i or SimDTM/SimJTAG Operation: issue DMI request/response transactions and trigger debug module controls Object: dm_top DMI-visible state and outputs ndmreset_o, dmactive_o, debug_req_o Expected guard: debug/JTAG authentication or authorization such as jtag_unlock/lifecycle/test authorization before DMI requests affect core debug controls or protected debug state Observed behavior: dmi_jtag receives jtag_hash/okey_hash/ikey_hash and emits jtag_unlock, but debug_req/debug_req_valid are wired from dmi_jtag/SimDTM toward dm_top; no provided evidence shows jtag_unlock gating dm_top DMI request consumption or dm_top outputs. td_o/tdo_oe_o are driven from dmi_jtag in non-simulation JTAG mode. Path: tck_i/tms_i/td_i -> dmi_jtag.dmi_req_o/dmi_req_valid_o -> debug_req/debug_req_valid -> dm_top -> ndmreset_o/dmactive_o/debug_req_o; RISCV_FESVR_SIM alternative: SimDTM -> debug_req/debug_req_valid -> dm_top Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: If dmi_jtag does not internally block unauthorized DMI traffic, an external debugger may access debug module state, request hart debug entry, or assert debug resets without the expected top-level authorization gate.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full dm_top instantiation lines 354-388 and dmi_jtag internals were not included; authorization may be internal to dmi_jtag but is not proven by provided evidence.

findings.summary: reglk_wrapper treats jtag_unlock as part of its reset/clear condition and clears all lock memory to zero, apparently removing locks globally.
findings.vulnerability_category: unlock/override lock bits and subsequently write locked registers / reglk_ctrl_o/reglk_ctrl lock control state consumed by protected peripheral wrappers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: JTAG/debug path asserting jtag_unlock, or AXI/NOC software writing reglk registers Operation: unlock/override lock bits and subsequently write locked registers Object: reglk_ctrl_o/reglk_ctrl lock control state consumed by protected peripheral wrappers Expected guard: jtag_unlock must be authenticated and must not clear/override sticky register locks except under explicitly authorized debug/lifecycle state Observed behavior: reglk_wrapper reset condition is ~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9); when true it clears reglk_mem entries to 'h0. Protected writes use lock bits as reglk bit ? old : wdata, indicating zero means writable/unlocked. No additional lifecycle/debug authorization is visible at the clear site. Path: dmi_jtag.jtag_unlock_o -> riscv_peripherals.jtag_unlock -> reglk_wrapper.jtag_unlock -> reglk_mem cleared -> reglk_ctrl_o/reglk_ctrl -> wrappers' reglk_ctrl_i -> protected write enables Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A jtag_unlock pulse can remove lock protection for multiple protected peripherals, enabling later writes to HMAC, fuse/PKT, access-control, reset, DMA, AES/RSA and other registers if unlock is not securely authorized elsewhere.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: dmi_jtag internal authentication of jtag_unlock and exact top-level reglk_wrapper instantiation lines are partly from model observation/truncated content, not full source excerpt.

findings.summary: HMAC secret/intermediate registers are readable through the AXI rdata mux when corresponding reglk read-lock bits are clear; key material is protected only by lock bits/acct gating, and debug_mode_i zeroizes engine inputs but not necessarily MMIO readback.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/configure HMAC key, hash-bypass, intermediate hash, result, and control/data registers / HMAC key0, key_hash_bypass, ikey_hash_bytes, okey_hash_bytes, hash/result and start controls / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/MMIO requester through HMAC NOC/AXI-lite bridge Operation: read/write/configure HMAC key, hash-bypass, intermediate hash, result, and control/data registers Object: HMAC key0, key_hash_bypass, ikey_hash_bytes, okey_hash_bytes, hash/result and start controls Expected guard: HMAC key/control registers should be writable/readable only when allowed by reglk_ctrl_i/acct_ctrl_i and appropriate privilege/access policy; secret key material should not be readable through rdata/debug paths Observed behavior: Wrapper gates AXI enable with acct_ctrl_i and uses reglk_ctrl_i bits in write/read cases. Writes to key0/ikey/okey are allowed when reglk_ctrl_i[5] is clear. Read mux returns ikey_hash_bytes and okey_hash_bytes when reglk_ctrl_i[4] is clear, and hash words when reglk_ctrl_i[6] is clear. debug_mode_i zeroes key and okey_hash inputs to the HMAC core, but provided readback evidence does not show debug_mode_i suppressing rdata of stored key/intermediate registers. Path: HMAC NOC bridge -> hmac_axi_req -> hmac_wrapper axi_lite_interface -> en/we/address decode -> key0/ikey_hash_bytes/okey_hash_bytes/hash registers -> rdata -> AXI response Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Secret or derived key material may be exposed to authorized-by-acct but otherwise unprivileged MMIO requesters when read-lock bits are not set; debug_mode_i does not demonstrate readback suppression.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full hmac read mux lines for key0 and all control registers were truncated; exact address map and whether key0 itself is readable were not fully observed.

findings.summary: pkt_wrapper allows fuse request/address writes under acct_ctrl_i but without reglk_ctrl_i write protection, and directly exposes fuse_rdata_i through rdata when enabled and at address 4 if reglk_ctrl_i[6] is clear.
findings.vulnerability_category: request/read fuse data or configure fuse address / fuse_req_o, fuse_addr_o, fuse_rdata_i, pkey_loc, and AXI rdata / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/MMIO requester through pkt_wrapper Operation: request/read fuse data or configure fuse address Object: fuse_req_o, fuse_addr_o, fuse_rdata_i, pkey_loc, and AXI rdata Expected guard: fuse requests and exposure of fuse_rdata must be mediated by access-control/lock policy and allowed only for authorized subjects/addresses Observed behavior: pkt_wrapper gates en with acct_ctrl_i. On en && we, address[7:3] 0 writes fuse_req_o <= wdata[0] and address 1 writes fuse_addr_o <= wdata, with no reglk_ctrl_i check in write side. Read side defaults rdata to fuse_rdata_i whenever en, and address 4 returns fuse_rdata_i unless reglk_ctrl_i[6] is set. pkey_loc halves are returned when reglk_ctrl_i[4]/[5] are clear. Path: PKT NOC bridge -> pkt_axi_req -> pkt_wrapper axi_lite_interface -> en/we/address decode -> fuse_req_o/fuse_addr_o -> fuse_mem/fuse_rdata_i -> rdata/AXI response Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Authorized-by-acct requesters may drive arbitrary fuse reads and observe fuse data/pkey location unless separate lock bits are pre-set and maintained; request/address path lacks lock enforcement.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Top-level pkt_wrapper instantiation lines immediately connecting fuse_req/fuse_addr/fuse_rdata were not fully included, though nearby fuse_mem connectivity confirms the fuse signals.

findings.summary: Access-control policy can be globally forced open by we_flag in acct_wrapper output composition, and reset defaults set acct_mem to all ones; some peripheral bridges lack visible top-level acc_ctrl gating except inside selected wrappers.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure access-control policy and access peripherals under that policy / acc_ctrl_o/acc_ctrl/acc_ctrl_c and protected peripheral request enables / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/MMIO software requester configuring policy; bus masters accessing peripherals Operation: configure access-control policy and access peripherals under that policy Object: acc_ctrl_o/acc_ctrl/acc_ctrl_c and protected peripheral request enables Expected guard: only authorized/privileged software should modify access-control matrix; acc_ctrl updates should be locked/mediated; all protected request paths should use access-control decisions Observed behavior: acct_wrapper outputs acc_ctrl_o with acct_mem[0] OR {8{we_flag}}, meaning we_flag can set policy bits regardless of stored policy. Reset initializes acct_mem entries to 32'hffffffff, apparently allowing all access by default. Top-level derives acc_ctrl_c with a special alias for j==5 and passes acct_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][index] into many wrappers, but CLINT/PLIC NOC bridge excerpts do not show acc_ctrl gating. Boot ROM has an explicit rom_req gate. Path: acct NOC bridge -> acct_wrapper -> acct_mem/we_flag -> acc_ctrl_o -> riscv_peripherals acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][peripheral] -> wrapper acct_ctrl_i or explicit request gates Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Policy root may fail closed-open safety: reset defaults and we_flag can authorize accesses broadly, and any path not wired to acct_ctrl_i/acc_ctrl_c remains outside the policy.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full list of all peripheral gates was not available; CLINT/PLIC excerpts may be intentionally unprotected, but expected policy coverage is not proven.

findings.summary: The top-level uses per-peripheral NOC-to-AXI-lite bridges that can drive wrapper AXI requests directly; acc_ctrl gating is present in many wrapper acct_ctrl_i ports but absent in some visible bridge/request paths, and the full decode/overlap policy is not evidenced.
findings.vulnerability_category: route/read/write MMIO requests to peripherals / protected peripheral MMIO regions and request enables / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CPU/AXI/NOC requester entering riscv_peripherals Operation: route/read/write MMIO requests to peripherals Object: protected peripheral MMIO regions and request enables Expected guard: each protected peripheral decode/request path must be gated by acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][peripheral] and any lock/debug/test policy; no unguarded alias/range overlap should reach protected wrappers Observed behavior: Many protected wrappers receive acct_ctrl_i from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][index], including HMAC, RST, RSA, AES, PKT/ACCT/REGLK/DMA by observations. However, NOC bridge valid signals feed per-peripheral AXI requests directly, and CLINT/PLIC excerpts show bridge connections without acc_ctrl_c gating. Boot ROM has explicit rom_req gating. No comprehensive centralized address decode/range overlap evidence is provided; instead distinct NOC ports target each peripheral. Path: peripheral-specific NOC ports -> noc_axilite_bridge -> wrapper/module AXI request signals -> internal wrapper access-control or unguarded module request handling Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Protected MMIO access may depend on inconsistent per-wrapper guards; any omitted wrapper or direct bridge can bypass acc_ctrl policy.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full top-level from 0-1980 is partially truncated; exact address map, default response behavior, and all wrapper instantiations were not fully observed.

findings.summary: Reset wrapper can assert local resets for protected wrappers under acc_ctrl/lock policy, and those local resets clear security state such as reglk_mem, acct_mem, HMAC state, and fuse request/address; if reset writes are authorized or policy is open by default, resets can clear locks/policies/secrets.
findings.vulnerability_category: trigger peripheral reset or clear security-relevant state / rst_1..rst_13, wrapper reset inputs rst_4/rst_5/rst_6/rst_9, lock/access-control/secret state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: MMIO/NOC software requester or reset peripheral Operation: trigger peripheral reset or clear security-relevant state Object: rst_1..rst_13, wrapper reset inputs rst_4/rst_5/rst_6/rst_9, lock/access-control/secret state Expected guard: reset triggers for security-sensitive peripherals should be privileged/authorized and should not clear lock/access-control state or leave stale secret data observable Observed behavior: rst_wrapper is connected to NOC through rst_axi_req/resp and receives reglk_ctrl_i/acct_ctrl_i/debug_mode_i. It outputs rst_1..rst_13. HMAC reset rst_4 clears control/data/key state, PKT reset rst_5 clears fuse_req/fuse_addr, ACCT reset rst_6 initializes acct_mem to all ones, and REGLK reset rst_9 clears reglk_mem to zero. The source logic inside rst_wrapper was not provided, but top-level demonstrates the reset-control path and security-state effects. Path: RST NOC bridge -> rst_axi_req -> rst_wrapper -> rst_4/rst_5/rst_6/rst_9 -> hmac_wrapper/pkt_wrapper/acct_wrapper/reglk_wrapper state reset Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A requester that can access reset control can reset away lock and access-control restrictions or alter security state, potentially bypassing sticky protections and returning policy to permissive defaults.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: rst_wrapper implementation and its write authorization semantics were not provided, so whether reset triggering is adequately protected is uncertain.

findings.summary: The lock-policy root is AXI-accessible and can be reset/cleared to unlocked values by rst_9 or jtag_unlock; lock bits are not sticky against these clears.
findings.vulnerability_category: write/read/configure or clear lock registers / reglk_ctrl_o lock bits and local reglk_mem/reglk_ctrl state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/MMIO requester configuring reglk_wrapper, reset controller, or JTAG unlock path Operation: write/read/configure or clear lock registers Object: reglk_ctrl_o lock bits and local reglk_mem/reglk_ctrl state Expected guard: register-lock configuration registers must be protected against unauthorized writes, cannot be cleared after lock unless authorized, and should have safe reset defaults Observed behavior: reglk_wrapper clears all reglk_mem to zero on reset/rst_9/jtag_unlock. AXI writes are allowed when en=en_acct&&acct_ctrl_i and selected root lock bits permit; readback of lock registers is hidden only when reglk_ctrl[0] is set. There is no evidence of sticky set-only semantics; wdata can overwrite lock memory when permitted. Path: REGLK NOC bridge -> reglk_axi_req -> reglk_wrapper address/en/we -> reglk_mem -> reglk_ctrl_o; jtag_unlock/rst_9 reset path clears reglk_mem Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Untrusted software with reset/JTAG access, or software before locking, can rewrite or clear the lock root, undermining downstream peripheral protections.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full read side and exact top-level reglk_wrapper instantiation lines before 1840 were not fully included; write intent and reset polarity semantics assumed from provided code.

findings.summary: External we_flag/debug/test inputs influence security controls and protected wrappers; we_flag_0 can force acc_ctrl bits, we_flag_1 is ORed into AES0 reglk_ctrl_i, we_flag_3 reaches DMA, and we_flag_4 reaches dmi_jtag, with no lifecycle/production guard evidenced at top level.
findings.vulnerability_category: override or enable writes via test/debug/write-enable flags / protected wrapper registers, acc_ctrl policy, reglk_ctrl policy, HMAC/debug controls, DMA/AES controls / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: test/debug environment or external integration driving mode/flag inputs Operation: override or enable writes via test/debug/write-enable flags Object: protected wrapper registers, acc_ctrl policy, reglk_ctrl policy, HMAC/debug controls, DMA/AES controls Expected guard: test/debug mode and external write-enable flags must not bypass normal register locks/access-control in production unless secure lifecycle/debug authorization is present Observed behavior: Top-level exposes testmode_i, debug_mode_i, we_flag_0..4 as inputs. dmi_jtag consumes testmode_i and we_flag_4. acct_wrapper ORs we_flag into acc_ctrl_o. AES0 wrapper receives reglk_ctrl slice OR we_flag_1. DMA wrapper receives we_flag_3. HMAC receives debug_mode_i and zeroizes some core inputs, but debug_mode_i effects elsewhere are wrapper-specific. No secure lifecycle guard for these external inputs is shown. Path: top-level mode/flag inputs -> dmi_jtag/acct_wrapper/aes0_wrapper/dma_wrapper/hmac_wrapper/rst_wrapper -> authorization/write-enable or data-path effects Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Externally driven test/debug/write-enable flags may bypass or alter normal policy/lock enforcement in production integration.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Intent of each we_flag and lifecycle source constraints are not included; AES0 ORing with we_flag_1 may lock rather than unlock depending downstream bit semantics, but it is still an unguarded external policy input.

findings.summary: Top-level instantiates dma_wrapper with acct_ctrl_i(acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8]), reglk_ctrl_i, pmpcfg_i, pmpaddr_i, we_flag_3, and rst_8. A NOC AXI-lite bridge exposes DMA configuration via ariane_dma NOC ports. However, the internal dma_wrapper implementation, active transfer master path, and PMP/access checks are not provided.
findings.vulnerability_category: DMA read/write or route DMA NOC transactions / DMA-accessible memory/peripheral regions, protected wrappers, access-control policy / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: DMA engine/NOC DMA requester Operation: DMA read/write or route DMA NOC transactions Object: DMA-accessible memory/peripheral regions, protected wrappers, access-control policy Expected guard: DMA-originated accesses should be subject to the same address-range/access-control policy as CPU accesses, with no missing bounds or privilege-attribute bypass Observed behavior: Top-level instantiates dma_wrapper with acct_ctrl_i(acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8]), reglk_ctrl_i, pmpcfg_i, pmpaddr_i, we_flag_3, and rst_8. A NOC AXI-lite bridge exposes DMA configuration via ariane_dma NOC ports. However, the internal dma_wrapper implementation, active transfer master path, and PMP/access checks are not provided. Path: buf_ariane_dma_noc2_* -> dma NOC AXI-lite bridge -> dma_axi_req/resp -> dma_wrapper; internal DMA transfer path not shown Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: Cannot determine whether DMA-originated accesses are constrained to authorized ranges or whether DMA can access protected peripheral/memory regions.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: dma_wrapper source and any DMA master transaction path/address range enforcement are not included in facts or observations.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
